title: Online Fraud and Cheating Case Under Bangladesh Law: Your Legal Options
metaTitle: Online Fraud Bangladesh – Legal Action for E-commerce and Digital Cheating
metaDescription: Cheated online in Bangladesh? Learn how to file a case for e-commerce fraud, advance fee scams, and digital cheating under the Penal Code and Digital Security Act.
heroIntro: Online fraud is surging in Bangladesh — fake e-commerce shops, advance fee scams, fake job offers, and investment fraud are causing significant financial losses. Bangladesh law provides multiple legal routes to pursue both criminal prosecution and civil recovery of your money. This guide walks you through your options.
toc: What is Online Fraud Under Bangladesh Law?
Common Types of Online Fraud in Bangladesh
Laws That Apply to Online Fraud
How to File a Fraud Case

sections.h2: What is Online Fraud Under Bangladesh Law?
sections.content:
Online fraud involves using digital channels — websites, social media, messaging apps — to deceive a person into parting with money or valuables under false pretences. Under Bangladesh law, this falls under:

  • Penal Code 1860, Section 420: Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property — up to 7 years' imprisonment and fine.
  • Digital Security Act 2018, Section 25: Publishing false, menacing, or offensive information through digital means — up to 5 years.
  • DSA Section 24: Identity theft and impersonation online — up to 5 years.

Contact a cyber law lawyer in Dhaka to determine which laws apply to your specific fraud situation.

sections.h2: Common Types of Online Fraud in Bangladesh
sections.content:
The most reported online fraud types in Bangladesh include:

  • Fake e-commerce shops: Facebook pages or websites that take payment and never deliver goods.
  • Advance fee scams: Promises of large sums of money, lottery wins, or job offers requiring upfront payment.
  • Investment fraud: Fake investment platforms (cryptocurrency, stock trading) promising unrealistic returns.
  • Impersonation fraud: Scammers posing as government officials, bank employees, or trusted persons to extract money.
  • Romance scams: Building fake romantic relationships online to eventually extort money.
  • Fake job offers: Charging fees for non-existent jobs, especially targeting overseas employment seekers.

sections.h2: How to File a Fraud Case
sections.content:
Steps to take after falling victim to online fraud:

  1. Collect and preserve all evidence: Screenshots of conversations, payment receipts, the website/page URL, bank transfer records, and any contact information used by the fraudster.
  2. File an FIR at your nearest police station under sections 420 of the Penal Code and relevant DSA provisions.
  3. Report to the Cyber Crime Unit of the Bangladesh Police (Cyber Support for Women Desk for women victims) or file directly at the Cyber Tribunal.
  4. Report to your bank immediately if you made a bank transfer — they may be able to freeze the recipient's account if you act within 24-48 hours.
  5. File a civil suit for recovery of the fraudulently obtained money.

Contact Advocate Md. Shah Alam in Uttara for immediate legal action on online fraud cases.

Recovery is possible but depends on speed of action:

  • Bank transfers (within 24-48 hours): Contact your bank and the fraudster's bank immediately to request a freeze. Courts can compel banks to reverse fraudulent transactions if acted upon quickly.
  • Mobile banking fraud (bKash/Nagad): Report immediately to the MFS provider — they have fraud departments and can sometimes reverse transactions if the money has not been withdrawn.
  • Court recovery order: Once a criminal case is filed and assets of the fraudster are identified, courts can order attachment and recovery.

The single most important factor in financial recovery is speed — every hour of delay reduces the chance of recovery significantly.

faqs.question: Can I get my money back from a Facebook page scam?
faqs.answer: If the Facebook page operator can be identified and their bank account or MFS wallet traced, courts can order recovery. Reporting to police quickly and engaging a lawyer for an urgent court order gives the best chance of recovery.

faqs.question: Is it worth filing a case for a small fraud amount?
faqs.answer: Even small amounts should be reported — patterns of fraud by the same operator can be established across multiple complaints, making prosecution much stronger. Filing a case also creates an official record that supports insurance or tax claims.

faqs.question: Can I sue a fake e-commerce website anonymously?
faqs.answer: You can file an FIR. The police or Cyber Tribunal can compel Facebook, domain registrars, and payment processors to reveal the owner's identity through court orders.
